observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous audit recommendation for the LCE to direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Assessor, and other concerned Department Heads to prepare and come up with Revised Revenue Code for the LGU. Thereafter, submit to the Sangguniang Bayan for approval the proposed Revised Local Tax Code.

We recommended that the LCE direct the OIC-MA to prepare the corresponding JEVs to capitalize the borrowing costs totaling ₱8.11 million of each project funded from the aforecited loans and disclosed the necessary details in the Notes to FS as at December 31, 2018. Also, to furnish the Audit Team with the JEVs, Loan Agreements with the financial institutions and the updated subsidiary ledgers for reference in audit.

We recommended that Management direct the MA to prepare an Analysis of the Government Equity Account as supporting disclosures in the CY 2017 Consolidated Notes to Financial Statements at year-end and henceforth, prepare the same.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to conduct immediate reconciliation of the individual balances of the Cash-in Bank account with the pertinent Cashbooks and the Cash-in Bank accounts and to make the necessary adjustments thereon.
Further, direct the Municipal Accountant to record the reconciling items in the books of accounts based on the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) prepared. Moreover, for the Municipal Accountant to also furnish the Municipal Treasurer copies of the BRS for the recording of reconciling items, if any, pertaining to her records.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to review and verify the collectability of the dormant receivables and request for authority to write-off those found to be uncollectible, in accordance with procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005.
Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant shall conduct regular monitoring and analysis of receivables to ensure that these are timely collected when the accounts become due and demandable.

Management acknowledged the above observation and explained that the LGU is currently implementing the periodic inventory method as they deem it more applicable. Directly issuing supplies and inventory to end-users, upon receipt, since they do not have the space for safe-keeping them and due to lack of personnel to monitor these inventories.
Management will, however, implement the above audit recommendations starting CY 2022.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that:
The Municipal Accountant to: a) Fully transfer the remaining balance of the Local Road Network from the Registry of Public Infrastructure to the LGU’s books of accounts; b) Maintain complete and updated Local Road Network Ledger Cards (LRNLCs) for all roads and road components; and c) Prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation for each road component at the end of the year;
The Municipal General Services Officer to keep complete and updated Local Road Network Property Cards (LRNPCs) for all roads and its components; and
The Municipal Engineer to a) Conduct complete physical inventory of all road networks of the Municipality and determine its proper valuation by reference to existing records/contracts or thru appraisal thereof; and b) To provide the Municipal Accountant and the MGSO with the complete description and cost segregation of the road components for all road networks as basis for recording in the subsidiary records.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant prepare an aging of accounts receivables, make the final reconciliation and accounting of these account balances and prepare the DVs together with the supporting schedule for the Municipal Treasurer to issue a check payable either to the Source Agency or direct to the National Treasury.
We further recommend that remaining balances which are considered as special purpose funds should be retained and fund balances corresponding to original releases such as financial assistance or subsidy, as well as funds which the disposition of the unused balance is not specified, maybe reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the Municipality for future utilization thereof subject to the usual accounting, auditing and budgeting laws and regulations.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue the timely submission of the year-end financials statements, together with its supporting schedules, to enable the timely verification thereof by the Auditor.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue to improve his performance in the submission of the monthly trial balances and the quarterly financial statements.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management:
Require the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the abovementioned existing law, rules and regulations;
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as applicable; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Accountant hasten the preparation of the BRS and furnish a copy thereof to the Audit Team, together with the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs), paid checks and original copies of Debit and Credit Memos for review and verification.

We recommended that the Management monitor and see to it that fidelity bonds of all its accountable officers are renewed on or before its validity date, as required in the Bureau of Treasury Order No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009.

We recommended that the Municipality adhere to the mandatory posting on the GPPB Online Portal of information relative to Emergency Procurements under the Bayanihan Act, to ensure that accountability and transparency in the procurement is upheld.

We recommended that Management strictly abide by the RA 9184 and its Revised IRR in their procurement process, and specifically stop the practice of referring to brand names in preparing Purchase Request (PR); instead they should indicate relevant specifications and/or performance requirement of the supplies/ materials and equipment needed.
Management Comment:
Management duly acknowledges the audit observation and will immediately stop the practice of referring to brand names in preparing the purchase request, and henceforth, strictly abide by specific sections of the RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the LCE provide justification why the mandatory requirement for a CPES is not observed.  Henceforth, we enjoin LCE to revisit the provisions as provided under Section 13 of the Annex “E” of the R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR on the evaluation of contractor’s performance.  Further, that concerned department representatives be convened to discuss on the subject matter to ascertain commitment/s in terms of agency resources towards conformance to the mandatory requirement for a CPES and its necessary impact to the operations and structure of the LGU.

We recommended that Management must ensure the following:
That the surety bond issued by contractors as performance bonds on infrastructure projects shall contain the specific dates of coverage, specifically the date starting from the effectivity of the bond up to the time of completion of the project and up to one year from completion, including the expiration date thereof. Henceforth, surety bonds posted by contractors with inconsistent or unclear stipulations relative to the validity period are not accepted as Performance Bond.
That projects with approved contract time extensions are supported with evidence that the validity of the performance security is correspondingly extended pursuant to Section 11.5 of Annex “E” of the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to release the retention money for infrastructure projects in accordance to Section 6.2 of the “Annex E” of the IRR of RA 9184.
Henceforth, compel all the contractors of the infrastructure projects of the Municipal Government  whose defects liability period had not yet expired and whose retention money were released prior to the lapse of the one year period after completion or was not deducted to the first and final billing of 100% completed projects, as the case maybe, without a valid substitute equivalent to the retention money as prescribed in the IRR of RA 9184, to post the required financial instrument that is valid until the end of the defects liability of the projects, otherwise, demand that they refund or pay the retention money for the projects.
Further, to instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to remind the bidders for the contracts to be bidded by the Municipal Government of the requirement of retention money on projects as provided in the above cited provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.
We recommended that the Local DRRM Office be institutionalized and organized according to law by assigning full-time permanent employees thereat to maximize its efficiency and effectiveness.

Further, we reiterated our previous recommendations that Management:
Require the Municipal Planning and Development Office and Municipal Engineer’s Office to fast track the implementation of the various projects in order to achieve the desired outcomes which are essential in the attainment of its goals and objectives in improving the quality of life of its constituents;
Maximize the utilization of funds allocated for the implementation of various projects that are envisioned to help in the attainment of social, economic and environmental development during the year; and
Adopt strategies, despite personnel limitations, so that planned projects by the LGU may be timely implemented within the budget year and completed within the stipulated time frames.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management, through the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) facilitate the full  establishment of the GAD database in order to have a basis for planning and programming PPAs and formulating policies that are gender-responsive and to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on relevant data to properly identify existing gender issues and ensure that the approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GAD Plan and Budget are gender responsive.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management establish a Monitoring and Evaluation System to enable it to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of its gender mainstreaming efforts.
We recommended that the GAD Focal Person submit the CY 2021 GAD Plan and Budget as well the as GAD Accomplishment Report to the Audit Team for evaluation and verification.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management prepare and submit the reports and outputs enumerated in DILG MC No. 2012-120 by the end of current year, copy furnished the Audit Team, for the proper and effective implementation of LCPC programs, projects and activities.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to review and verify the collectability of the dormant receivables and request for authority to write-off those found to be uncollectible, in accordance with procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

Henceforth, the OIC-Municipal Accountant shall conduct regular monitoring and analysis of receivables to ensure that these are timely collected when the accounts become due and demandable.
